content: We (Nova, Tyr) need support for call_rcu(). Anyone working on infrastructure for that already?
my understanding is it was not yet added due to lack of usecase. @Boqun Feng will have more context.

I think Alice has some POC for her own usage: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260116-rcu-box-v1-1-38ebfbcd53f0@google.com/

The question is: do Nova and Tyr want to use RcuBox or the objects being call_rcu() on have their own rcu_head.
The latter

I see, so likely you will need to play the same "intrusive data" game as hrtimer and work_struct, I do have a patchset to consolidate this: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260128215330.58410-1-boqun.feng@gmail.com/ so that you won't need to do impl_has_rcu_head!() for your types.

Are the object types defined at C side?

No, it's our own pure Rust data

We want to defer dropping DriverFence::data because C callbacks (guarded with RCU) might still be accessing it (the callbacks being our Rust versions, but doing container_of on the C embedded structure)

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/pstanner/linux-drm-work/-/blob/jobqueue-jobqueue-rfc-v3-3/rust/kernel/sync/dma_fence.rs?ref_type=heads#L465
Does the C fence struct already have an rcu_head field?
It has that, but doesn't seem to be available before we drop the refcount to 0
Ok, but are we not using it in the same case as when it would be used in C?

No. It's only used to perform kfree_rcu() if there's no provided release() function (as far as I can tell).

We want to use it to defer freeing data until every potential reader has seen that we signaled the fence
I thought the readers can't access the Rust data to start with?

Well yes, but actually no :sweat_smile:

The issue is that sooner or later someone will want to add support for the more exotic dma_fence callbacks. We (I) currently only support the mandatory ones, get_timeline_name() and get_driver_name(), whose life time issues we can annihilate completely with owned strings.

But more exotic callbacks are sooner or later going to be implemented in Rust, and there C just returns the embedded C struct.

But the Rust driver will most likely need its generic data T in the Rust callback. So we have to pass it over.

And now what might happen that a different code path of the Rust driver drops that data, and the C backend_ops mechanism has no awareness of it, and then you get a UAF in Rust
Thus, dropping data needs to be defered (RCU) until the C callbacks definitely cannot see data anymore, i.e. until after the fence has signaled
But if you provide any such access to the private data, doesn't that cause issues with module unloading?
Not if we guarantee that all fences get signaled before the module unloads, which we now want to do (even if Christian doesn't like that, but he can be convinced) :slight_smile:

While that sounds like a fine thing to require, it sounds very difficult to enforce that strictly enough that you can rely on it being true in unsafe code. For that to be the case, it needs to be impossible to bypass it in safe code, no matter how stupid that safe code is.
To give an example, what if I call mem::forget on my driver fence or fence context?

While that sounds like a fine thing to require, it sounds very difficult to enforce that strictly

I can't follow. What is the "that" and the "it" in this sentence?
You mean enforcing signalling on drop?
Yes
Or, rather, enforcing signalling prior to module unload.
Unsafe code cannot rely on drop being called. For example, maybe the driver has a refcount cycle that prevents the value from being cleaned up.
Do you mean that a driver can unload without all Rust data being dropped automatically, without the driver doing something very stupid?
Sure, the driver author could accidentally have a refcounting cycle that leads to cleanup not happening.

Yes, but the driver would than circumvent our intended design. There are reasons why we do not give drivers a DriverFence as an Arc. Like that reason for instance.

That needs to be documented of course. Not dropping all your fences on unload is a bug and leads to undefined behavior.

At a certain point, you just have to trust the drivers. Drivers could also unload their module, clean up everything, but forget to switch off the GPU, which will then blow up the entire computer through DMA

But I'm open to hear alternative solutions, of course

We have to distinguish between "if this happens, it would be a bug" and "I'm sufficiently confident that this won't happen that I am going to rely on it in unsafe code". At first glance, "fence must be signalled prior to module unload" sounds like the kind of thing that is in the first category, but not the second category.

Maybe there is some way to put it in the second strategy, but I would need to hear how its enforced to feel confident about it.

To make an analogy, the red/black tree obviously requires that the elements are stored in sorted order. Otherwise it can't find elements when you perform a look-up. However, the end user could create an element containing a mutex that lets you change its value without moving its location in the tree. The end-user could utilize this to create a red/black tree that is out of order, and the red/black tree abstraction can do nothing to prevent that.

So because of this, I designed the red/black tree so that user code doing the above will not lead to undefined behavior. It will lead to it not functioning correctly. But not UB.

Philipp Stanner said:

Yes, but the driver would than circumvent our intended design. There are reasons why we do not give drivers a DriverFence as an Arc. Like that reason for instance.

I don't mean that the Driver fence itself would be an Arc, but that the Driver would put it in a struct and that struct is in an Arc

Ack, I understand the problem.

Sounds like something we could brainstorm more about in our Monday meeting if you've got time to join
I'd be happy to, but unfortunately I'm travelling to the US for a conference next week.
I can join the week after.

Hmm, so I'm not the great Rust expert, but to brainstorm a bit:

Would it be possible to create a type constraint / container type that cannot be stuffed into a refcounted container?
No, if you hand the user a value, then it's possible for the user to throw away the value without running drop. You can't prevent this.
But even if that's possible, it's probably not doable in practice because we cannot that drastically constrain a driver from where it wants to store its fences, I believe

Why is it that the receiver side of the fence needs to touch the user data? I recall from previous discussions that we concluded it was not required, and the fact that it's not required was the only reason we could stop using the two layers of fences.

I think if touching the user data / function pointers defined by the driver is required, then you have to go back to the two-fence solution where only the second fence is safe to outlive the module unload.

Well, that's a bit CS-Philosophy now

The consumer fence, or rather its user, doesn't need the user data and doesn't know about it.

Through the backend_ops callbacks, it more or less asks the producer of the fence (the driver, the owner of the DriverFence) about the state of that fence. And for this the producer again needs the data, probably

We are currently discussing a two-fence solution. See my code linked above.

The difference since a few weeks ago is just that both fences now live in the same chunk of memory; yet their accessability life time is still decoupled

I think that to satisfy the requirement that the module can be unloaded even if there are still consumers of the fence, then it's simply not possible for the consumer to call any function that is in the module. The consumer will only be able to access information for which we can write generic code in the core kernel.

I guess, looking over the code and such, a bigger question to me is the struct dma_fence_ops value. This is a value in static memory and if any of the function pointers are per-driver, then the entire ops value is also going to be part of the module.

So you can't even access the ops value itself from a receiver if that is the case.
I guess I'm a bit sceptical of the claim that we can implement fences that can survive module unload without further changes to the C side ...

The C guys are already solving exactly that problem (I think that was merged recently, let me search…)

When a fence signals, the ops pointer in the fences gets set to NULL and ops access is guarded by RCU

As for "consumers access data":

I think that is just a reality. That is how the dma_fence contract works. Consumers must be able to ask the producers about what is the state of the job on the hardware

That cannot be abolished

then it's simply not possible for the consumer to call any function that is in the module

Yes it is – IF we only unload the module once all consumers are done with said accesses

yeah, here it is:
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/drm/misc/kernel/-/blob/drm-misc-next/drivers/dma-buf/dma-fence.c?ref_type=heads#L375
You can always rely on Christian :sunglasses:
The fences can survive module unload, just that it must be signaled so the op vtable are swapped out.

I think we should not focus too much on code UAF as it's basically not possible to prevent entirely with Rust, given that you can easily just hand out function pointers or &‘static STATIC, and none of them are actually 'static with the presence of module unload.
Code UAF is solved by C if I am not missing something
If you mean "programmer must not mess up" by "solve"

What about leaking kernel virtual addresses used by modules? Then the impact of code UAF is limited to a kernel oops.

Fixing this properly would require language changes or disabling module unload entirely.

@Philipp Stanner if you haven't started working on squashing Alice's patch and mine, I pushed a version at: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/boqun/linux.git/log/?h=rust-rcu, and I can send it out if you want (and if that could save you some time).
@Boqun Feng a new version you mean. I guess it contains the additional types that Danilo requested. So if you can send that, that would be helpful, yes. Thx.

Sent: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260605133541.22569-1-boqun@kernel.org/.

Although, I accidentally duplicated first two patches. Please use this one for RcuBox:

https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260605133541.22569-3-boqun@kernel.org/
